title: Postgres is not your read cache
tagline: 1 800 product reads a second, every one hitting Postgres. The read side is shedding 40% of them.
brief: You run a product catalog API: an edge LB fronts an API tier that queries Postgres for every GET /products/:id. A morning press feature tripled read traffic to 1 800 req/s — writes are still a trickle. The API tier is coasting. Postgres is not: the same hot products are being read over and over, and every single read goes to the database.
symptom: Watch Postgres's node card: errorRate pinned around 40% and p99 north of 600 ms, while the API tier sits near 60% CPU with zero errors. Reads arrive at 1 800/s against 1 000 reads/s of read capacity — 180% utilization, sustained. This isn't a burst; it never recovers on its own.

phases.description: Press feature steady state — 1 800 product reads/s

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Read-heavy product catalog. Hot working set is the press-featured products.

diagnosis.question: Postgres's error rate is pinned around 40% and its p99 is over 600 ms, but the API tier shows 0% errors and the write side is idle. What's the root cause?

options.text: Every product read goes straight to Postgres — 1 800 reads/s against 1 000 reads/s of read capacity, with no layer absorbing the repeat reads.
options.explain: Right. Read utilization = 1 800 ÷ 1 000 = 180%, and the database sheds the excess — that's the ~40% errorRate on the card. The same hot products are read over and over, which is precisely the workload a cache-aside layer absorbs: serve repeats from memory so only misses touch Postgres.

options.text: The connection pool (200) is exhausted — queries are queuing for connections and timing out. Raise connectionPoolSize.
options.explain: The card disagrees: activeConnections hovers near zero and the log never prints 'Connection pool exhaustion'. Postgres admits everything it receives within each second — it fails queries because the read side is past throughput capacity, and a bigger pool adds waiting room, not read throughput.

options.text: The API tier is saturated and dropping requests before they reach Postgres — add API instances.
options.explain: The API tier's card shows ~60% CPU and a 0% error rate: 15 instances at 5 ms/request is 3 000 rps of capacity against 1 800 arriving. The errors live on Postgres's card, downstream of the servers — adding API instances would only push the same doomed reads harder.

options.text: Product lookups are doing full table scans — add an index to products and the latency and errors disappear.
options.explain: The schema already has a btree on products.id, and the log never fires the 'unindexed access' callout that scan traffic triggers. The latency here tracks utilization, not scan factor — and an index cannot add read throughput once arrivals exceed capacity.

fix.objective: Get the repeat reads off Postgres: serve hot products from a layer in front of the database so reads stop failing and Postgres's p99 comes back down — without crashing anything.

criteria.label: Postgres stops failing reads (errors under 10% after warmup)
Postgres p99 under 400 ms after warmup

hints: Open Postgres's node card: writes are near zero and reads run at 180% of readThroughputRps (1 800/s vs 1 000/s). The same hot products are read over and over — what layer absorbs repeat reads?
Draw a cache between the API tier and the database: wire Product API → cache and cache → Postgres, then delete the direct Product API → Postgres wire so only misses touch the database.

Caches start cold and warm up over ~ttl/2 seconds. Set ttlSeconds to 60 so it's fully warm by t=30s — the default 1 GB easily holds the hot working set, and the read errors go to zero as the hit rate climbs.
